headline_metrics.nii: Q1 not stated as single figure; FY2026 total NII guided to approximately $103 billion, NII ex Markets approximately $95 billion
headline_metrics.nco_rate: Card net charge-off rate outlook approximately 3.4%

nim_nii.summary: Management held NII ex Markets guidance at about $95 billion and lowered total NII to about $103 billion as Markets NII falls to about $8 billion, driven predominantly by lower rates; asset sensitivity offset was small because rate changes were backdated.
nim_nii.quote: We now expect total NII to be approximately $103 billion as a function of market NII decreasing to about $8 billion, predominantly due to rates, which we expect will be primarily offset in NIR.
nim_nii.speaker: Jeremy Barnum, CFO

credit_normalization.summary: Consumer credit metrics look consistent with prior trends and fundamentally healthy, with the card NCO rate outlook held at approximately 3.4%; management attributes resilience mainly to a strong labor market.
credit_normalization.quote: Early roll rates, delinquency rates, cash buffer, spend, discretionary spend, non-discretionary spend, it all looks consistent with prior trends and fundamentally, healthy.
credit_normalization.speaker: Jeremy Barnum, CFO

cre_reserves.summary: JPM recorded a net reserve build of $191 million this quarter; weights were left unchanged and a lower weighted-average unemployment assumption created consumer tailwinds, with small builds in wholesale from loan growth and idiosyncratic downgrades. CRE/office was not specifically called out; management consciously decided not to add downside skew despite Middle East risks.
cre_reserves.quote: we actually did not change the weights this quarter.
cre_reserves.speaker: Jeremy Barnum, CFO

deposit_costs.summary: Deposit competition remains intense; interest-bearing deposit costs fell with the rate curve, and management expects the margin to stay roughly the same with limited room to price down the already-low-cost low-beta portion of the franchise.
deposit_costs.quote: The margin would be about what it is today, give or take, a couple of basis points up or down.
deposit_costs.speaker: James Dimon, Chairman & CEO

capital_return.summary: CET1 was 14.3% with about $40 billion of excess capital; management prefers to deploy capital serving clients over buybacks and would rather repurchase shares only at a real discount, while warning Basel III endgame and G-SIB reproposals would raise JPM's required capital.
capital_return.quote: Our preferred way of using capital is not buying back stock today. We're doing it, fair market value and all that, but I'd rather buy back stock when we think it's a real discount, and the ongoing shareholder gets the benefit of buying it cheap.
capital_return.speaker: James Dimon, Chairman & CEO

loan_growth.summary: Overall loan growth is modest outside Card (expected ~6% or a little more); this quarter's strong balance-sheet growth was primarily Markets-driven, low-density secured financing, with acquisition financing and data-center lending as opportunistic drivers held on balance sheet.
loan_growth.quote: But to a significant degree, some of that's going to get driven by acquisition financing, that we hold on balance sheet for a while, that some of that's a little bit of a driver this quarter as well.
loan_growth.speaker: Jeremy Barnum, CFO

macro_outlook.summary: Management views the consumer and economy as resilient but cautions that if a credit cycle comes it will be worse than people expect, with stress on leveraged companies refinancing; not deemed systemic but management is preparing for recession/stagflation.
macro_outlook.quote: if there's a credit cycle, I do expect it will be worse than people think relative to the scenario. It's not a disaster. We're used to the credit cycles. We'll be big boys about it. But asset prices will go down, credit spreads will going down. People may get a little nervous about some of those things.
macro_outlook.speaker: James Dimon, Chairman & CEO

notable_qa.summary: Gerard Cassidy pressed on how JPM would cut expenses over the next three quarters to hit the ~$105 billion guide; Barnum pushed back that the firm does not run around finding expenses to cut and that the $105 billion is an outcome of business results, with Dimon adding they would happily spend more if every quarter were this strong.
notable_qa.quote: The $105 billion is not a promise, it's an outcome of business results.
notable_qa.speaker: James Dimon, Chairman & CEO
